data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-04T00:51:52.667Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known EF Core change related to the Cosmos DB provider. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core, the Azure Cosmos DB provider has been moving toward async-only operations. In .NET 10 (EF Core 10), synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated but could still work with an opt-in configuration. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), the sync-over-async support for the Cosmos DB provider is expected to be fully removed.


The timeline shows how the Cosmos DB provider progressively restricted synchronous operations—starting with warnings in EF Core 9, moving to deprecation with opt-in workarounds in EF Core 10, and culminating in complete removal in EF Core 11, which means any code relying on synchronous LINQ queries against Cosmos DB will fail unless refactored to use async patterns. So the user's configuration that allowed sync operations in .NET 10 is completely gone in .NET 11—there's no way to opt back in anymore. Every synchronous LINQ method and database operation needs to switch to its async counterpart, and there's no escape hatch.
